sentences.text: My complete name is Roman Weingarten.
R-O-M-A-N W-E-I-N-G-A-R-T-E-N.
It was.
Never.
No.
Approaching 81.
In Krakow, Poland.
Krakow, in the Polish language is spelled K-R-A-K-O-W.
In English, it is C-R-A-C-O-W.
First, if you talk about Krakow, you have to have a little bit of a realization what Krakow was in history.
It was the, the most populated Jewish city and at the start, the very beginning of Krakow, in Poland.
And it was the cradle of Jewish culture and Jewish learning.
And I was brought in-- up in the tradition of that Jewish culture and Jewish learning.
My parents-- my father of course, was a follower of a rebbe.
Back here, you would know the [NON-ENGLISH], but this was somebody else.
And he was a very-- he's-- he was a-- and he brought us also to cherish this kind of, of life.
And we had a attended-- the rebbe had his own congregation.
And we used to attend services in his shul, so to speak.
It was not a big shul.
It was like more like a shtiebel that is known here.
And this is how we were, were brought up in that spirit.
The rebbe's name was [?
Erapshem ?] Klingberg.
He was the [NON-ENGLISH] rabbi.
Don't ask me to spell it in English.
But he was a very pious and learned man.
He was known to be a kabbalist.
And he took great pride and great interest in the young people in his congregation.
And I had many opportunities to speak to him one on one.
And he tried to influence me-- my, my behavior and all this.
And I, I, I have pictures of him from during the war.
And I cherish these pictures because I remember, if you would look at this picture, he is-- it was like you like a baby Moses.
He was not just a learned man, but a very dedicated, selfless person.
And as I said, we had the opportunity to, to talk one on one in his study.
And I was very much influenced him.
But still, I, I come from a family of six, and I'm the oldest.
And when at about 14 years of age, I became very much interested in secular learning.
And I was reading books which this rabbi probably wouldn't want me to do.
But you cannot go against nature.
And of course, in that way, I was the only one in my family who turned secular.
And all the others-- my two brothers and sisters, of which pictures you will see later, are still presenting themselves as the Hasidim.
They're wearing different garb, and they are bearded and all this.
And I'm the only one who-- maybe in their minds, maybe I was an outcast at this time.
But this is-- this is how it was with me.
Well, there were a large number of major synagogues, like the Alta shul, the Remuh shul, the Kupa shul, the Hoykh shul.
And these were major synagogues.
But besides that, there were small synagogues and shtiebel, mostly congregated by, by certain groups of people, mostly Hasidim.
Because I would say of the 60,000 Jews in Krakow, there are probably 90% were Hasidim, and only maybe 10% were secular.
And in fact, you didn't see a Jewish store open on a Saturday, even by, by the non-Hasidim because they were so influenced by the culture.
So everybody was for the-- for the appearance was a shomer Shabbas actually.
Oh yes.
The street where we lived was number four, Mostowa Street.
The, the building was-- had about 10 or 12 tenants.
In the back of it was a large factory building, which belonged to my parents and his-- he was in partnership with his brother and his father.
It was a chocolate factory.
The name of it was [POLISH].
It's a Polish name.
And what I remember about this-- I know that it's still standing there.
I still have pictures.
Somebody was there, and he brought me pictures of it.
It was an apartment in a building.
We had three rooms, and there were eight people living in it.
There were six children.
And we-- also there was a time that we had a maid, and there was room for everybody.
My father was very much involved in the production of that-- of the-- of the factory.
And in fact, by 1935, I also joined them, but I did office work.
And I became the head of the office of that company, so to speak.
His name is Josef, Josef Baruch.
Baruch means blessed.
And this Baruch was added to him when he was sick.
So his original name when he was born was just Josef, Joseph.
And when he was about 19 years old, he was very sick.
And he had an operation, appendicitis.
And at that time, appendicitis was really serious.
And then this rebbe, Klingberg, added this name, Baruch, to him to bless him.
And he recovered.
He was a rather stout person, but very good natured.
And though he-- we had some misunderstandings because of what I mentioned before, he still had this great understanding.
But I think the influence on him was my mother, actually because I was her favorite.
It's Pesel Fruma.
Yeah, two names.
Her maiden name is [?
Feuerstein. ?]
And she comes-- she came from a very small town about 50 miles from Krakow.
Actually, it was a village more than a town.
All her family lived there, and they dealt in timber.
All the Jews dealt in timber at this place.
And my father and mother were married in 1913.
And I was born in 1914 in Krakow.
Of course, after they were married, they made their residence in Krakow.
She was a lovely person.
I have a picture of her from 1920.
And if you see it, you will agree with me.
She had a great interest in music.
And though there was a-- she would not go to the opera, to a theatre-- which we had in Krakow operas at this time-- because of the background.
But she was interested to listen to the radio and to records.
Besides that, she was a very loving and caring person.
Every, every child of her was a single, was, was just for the sake of this particular child.
And I remember particularly, I think her affection for me was so outstanding [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] that I really choke up whenever I think of her.
I have two brothers, both younger than I am.
The first after me is Jechiel.
He is a Hasidic person.
He lives in Borough Park.
And he has a very nice extended family now.
He's very dedicated to tradition and to religion.
And, and his-- all his families are raised in that spirit.
The other brother, Yaakov, lives in Israel now, in Holon.
He's completely different than my brother in Brooklyn.
While before the war, they were all together, and they were going to yeshiva together and learning together.
After the war, they were like two different people.
The one in Brooklyn stayed Hasidic, and the other one became completely secular.
He also has-- he has two children, two daughters.
And he now has six grandchildren.
And they all live in Israel.
And they're very happy.
And in fact, I was in Israel only three weeks ago, when I saw the family.
Then we had-- I have two sisters-- I had three sisters, one of whom survived the Holocaust.
And her name is Rachel, 'Rah-kell,' and she lives in Queens.
And she's a widow now.
She has only one daughter and two grandchildren.
The other two sisters, Cerel and Rebecca perished in the Holocaust.
In fact, they were together with my parents when they were transported from the ghetto to some kind of a-- we assumed that it was Belzec that they were taken to.
Is Jakov,
Well as I said, until the age of about 14, I was walking in the footsteps of my family, of my father.
Not that I stopped admiring them later on, but my interest became more diversified.
And I was reading a lot of books, Polish books, which was not necessarily approved by this-- by this community that I was raised in.
I read all the-- most of the Polish classics.
I was very interested in poetry.
And I was-- since I did not go to secular school, I was preparing myself to get the equivalent of a high school diploma, which was called matura in Polish, by taking private lessons.
So I had very good tutors and eventually I've accomplished that.
The, the primary school was the cheder.
This was from the age of three years old.
And then I grew up.
And I joined the yeshiva, so to speak.
It was not an official yeshiva the way you know it here.
But it was every shtiebel, every beit midrash had somebody who was lecturing on Talmud and on Tanakh.
And this is where I was getting my education, my Jewish education, my Hebrew education.
I would say, well, if you-- if you figure that I started cheder when-- at the age of three, then it was-- I had probably 18 years of schooling.
But it all depends how much one can retain when he is three years old.
Oh, Shabbas was a special day.
Because all the problems, you, you tried to forget about everything that was going on that troubled you.
All problems were put aside.
Friday afternoon, we used to go to the mikvah, which it's maybe not in the same sense as you know a mikvah here.
But it was actually a Turkish bath.
And I used to love to go there in the Friday afternoon.
And you spend it for two hours.
And, and it's baths, Turkish baths.
And after this, we used to go to a place where you used to have a cold beer and something, some salami.
And this is how the Shabbat actually started.

And then in the evening, we would go to services, not unlike people here go to services Friday evening, except that the services were conducted in a completely different manner that you would know here.
It was Hasidic.
You would find it here also, in Borough Park.
It's approximately the same type of services.
Then after the services, in the evening, there was a very elaborate Shabbat dinner.
And noodles and chicken soup was on the table every Shabbat dinner, and chicken.
And while at the table, we're singing all kinds of songs, which in Hebrew Z'mirot.
And sometimes we used to get visitors come over.
The distances are very short.
People lived either next door or next street, and used to come over.
At other times, we would go to visit the others and spent a few hours Friday night like an oneg Shabbat that you know here.
Well, Shabbat morning, like every good Jew, goes to shul.
We went to the rebbe.
And we spent between three and four hours.
My father was also musically inclined.
And he was-- he had a good voice.
So sometimes he performed the services of the shaliach tzibbur, like the hazzan here, the cantor.
And this was not a profession for him.
It just was an amateurish thing.
In fact, I do this too today.
Yeah, well, Pesach didn't start Erev Pesach.
Pesach started two weeks before Pesach.
This is when you took apart the house.
Not only-- you were actually looking to clean every, every corner, every little-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know how to express this.
Anyway, it was something that it took, really, weeks to prepare for because we were very strict with regard to chametz, not to have chametz.
Not only the house, but all of the books that we had in our closets and on the shelves were taken out to the yard, And shaken in case that somebody would use it during a meal during the year.
And maybe some crumbs would get into it.
So we used to shake out these books so that they would be free of chametz.
And then of course a whole week, eight days of Pesach followed with all the traditions, with the-- with the seder, where we used to have all these people to come to our seder.
Invited them, people who didn't have families.
They would participate.
Of course, the children were all festively dressed.
And the table was decked with everything that we, we could muster, all silver.

Everything that we had in any place was on that table, at the seder, to show up the riches that we-- and then the connection, because the connection with the Exodus is such that when the Jews left Egypt, the ninth plague was complete darkness.
And this is a time when the Jews took all the, the silver and the gold and the valuable from the Egyptians.
And they carried it out from Egypt.
So on that account I think, we put everything on the table to show off.
Well, every holiday had a special meaning because OK, we are now on, on Pesach.
Then the next holiday to Pesach is Shavuot, which we just celebrated last week.
And this was a special holiday because this is remembering the giving of the Torah.
And this is celebrating the giving of the Torah and the other special, different traditions.
The-- all the synagogues are decorated with, with greens on that holiday.
It's not done here so much.
But still, in the Hasidic circles, like in Borough Park, you'll still find they take branches.
They cut the branches, fresh branches, and bring it into the shul to decorate it.
After this is-- comes Rosh Hashanah because then it's nothing between, just the summer.
And Rosh Hashanah is the new year, the Jewish new year.
Again, everything was-- the center of everything was the shul, and then the home when we came back.
These two places alternated with one another.

And there's nothing else in between because the people, the young people, even though they may have been bored, staying home or always learning, but they couldn't do anything else because of the restrictions put on them by families in the type of families that I come from.
I personally, I would say-- before we go through this, I want to continue with one other holiday, which is very important holiday, Sukkot.
Why's it important?
Because we really lived in the sukkah at this time, during the eight days.
Some slept in the sukkah also.
But we had all our meals in the sukkah.
And we spent all of free time in the sukkah.
The sukkah was, was a temporary home, which was made so comfortable that you really-- it was protected from the rain because we had these opening, the roofs.
In case it was raining, we could close the roof over it.
But while the roof was down, you are not allowed to eat there.
You could eat in the sukkah only when the roof was up.
And you were exposed to just the covering of, of the-- of the sukkah.
And we used to decorate the sukkah so magnificently, like it was like a castle.
You would have all kinds of figures, made of different colored paper hanging on the wall and hanging from the-- from the roof.
And the young people, I myself, used to spend weeks and weeks before Sukkot preparing all of these decoration.
It was a very special time because we were really into it.
And we took great pleasure and great pride in doing it.
Now you were asking me--
Yeah, I personally, yes.
Because we-- actually, here you would describe it the upper middle class.
Being in industrialists, we were financially pretty well to do.
But I had friends which came from different families.
And not all, but we all stuck together.
And if one didn't-- couldn't go to the movies because they didn't have the money, the others helped out.
So and I'm talking about the movies.
This was not that my brothers would do, but I would do it.
Oh, it will come back to me, not at this moment.
Well, I wouldn't call this a relationship because there was rampant anti-Semitism.
At this time, you're talking about 60 years ago, 65 years ago.

On a Sunday, when the-- particularly in the small towns and villages when the peasants came back from church, they were so aroused against the Jews because the sermons all concentrated on Jews, on killing Jesus.
And this was going on week after week.
When they came home from church on Sunday, they would kill the Jews instantly, if they were able to.
And the anti-Semitism in Poland was a rather ingrained anti-Semitism.
They were born.
A child is not being born prejudiced.
But they were so indoctrinated in this-- in these feelings from the very childhood that it stayed with them, and, and they couldn't get rid of it.
Even today, you can feel it, anti-Semitism without Jews.
And there is anti-Semitism in Poland.
No.
No.
No.
First of all, we were not directly involved.
In Krakow, you were not exposed to non-Jews the way you would be in a small town because you are-- you are living in a-- in a section, in a Jewish section.
And you were not simply-- you didn't have-- certainly, you didn't have Polish friends.
But you're not exposed to them the way that they were in the small towns.
Well, there was a time that the prime minister of Poland has made a speech.
And he said, in so many words, boycott against the Jews, yes, but no extreme violence.
So he was holding the people back from violence, but he said boycott is fine.
This came from the-- from the top.
